cards.front: Why were fixed-width integers introduced in C++11?
cards.back: To guarantee integer sizes across all systems, since standard types (int, long) can vary in size between platforms.

cards.text: Fixed-width integers are defined in the {{c1::<cstdint>}} header and use names like {{c2::std::int32_t}}.

cards.front: What are the common fixed-width signed integer types?

cards.text: Fixed-width integers are {{c1::optional}} - they are only defined if the system supports them with {{c2::no performance penalty}}.

cards.question: What's needed to use std::int32_t?
cards.explanation: Fixed-width integers are defined in the <cstdint> header, which must be included.

Use fixed-width integers when:
  • Storing data to file/network (guaranteed size)
  • Specific bit-width is required (e.g., 32-bit algorithm)
  • Platform independence is critical
Otherwise prefer int for performance.

cards.front: What are the downsides of fixed-width integers?
cards.back:
• Not guaranteed to be defined on all systems
  • May be slower than int on some architectures
  • Can cause unexpected behavior with integer promotion

cards.text: {{c1::std::int_fast32_t}} is the fastest signed integer type with at least 32 bits. {{c2::std::int_least32_t}} is the smallest with at least 32 bits.

cards.back: Unsigned integer type guaranteed to hold the size of the largest object. Returned by sizeof and used for array indexing.

cards.text: std::size_t is defined in the {{c1::<cstddef>}} header and is returned by the {{c2::sizeof}} operator.

cards.front: Why is using std::size_t for array indices problematic?
cards.back: Because it's unsigned, which can cause wraparound bugs in loops and comparisons. Prefer signed types even for indices.

cards.text: Best practice: Prefer {{c1::int}} for general purpose integers. Use {{c2::std::int_fast#_t}} when performance matters and size doesn't.
